Which of the following is the best definition of a virion?

An infective virus outside of a host


An inert virus outside of a host


An inert virus inside of a host

 

An infective virus inside of a host


A virus which has infected a bacteria
An infective virus outside of a host

Which of the following are present in ALL virions?

Capsomeres, nucleic acids, ribosomes, protomers, envelope


Ribosomes, nucleic acids, capsid, protomers


RNA, capsomeres, capsid, protomers, envelope

 

DNA, capsomeres, capsid, protomers, envelope


Nucleic acids, capsomeres, capsid, protomers
Nucleic acids, capsomeres, capsid, protomers

Which of the following are present in ALL virions?

Capsomeres, nucleic acids, ribosomes, protomers, envelope


Ribosomes, nucleic acids, capsid, protomers


RNA, capsomeres, capsid, protomers, envelope

 

DNA, capsomeres, capsid, protomers, envelope


Nucleic acids, capsomeres, capsid, protomers
Nucleic acids, capsomeres, capsid, protomers

A virus that infects a bacteria is known as a(n):

Virophage


Prokayrophage


Bacteriovirus

 

Eukaryophage


Bacteriophage
Bacteriophage

Why are scientists debating if viruses are living organisms?

They are so small.


They infect other pathogens.


They are metabolically inert.

 

They don't always have DNA.


They live inside a host.

One of the reasons why scientists debate whether viruses are living organisms is because viruses lack certain characteristics that are typically associated with living organisms. For example, viruses are typically much smaller than other organisms, and they do not have the ability to undergo metabolic processes on their own. Additionally, viruses cannot reproduce or carry out other life processes without infecting a host cell. However, they do possess nucleic acids and can evolve, which are characteristics of living organisms. The debate stems from the fact that viruses exhibit some but not all of the attributes typically used to define life.

Why are scientists debating if viruses are living organisms?

They are so small.


They infect other pathogens.


They are metabolically inert.

 

They don't always have DNA.


They live inside a host.

Scientists debate whether viruses are living organisms due to several reasons. Firstly, viruses are much smaller than typical cells, and their simple structure raises questions about their classification as living. Secondly, viruses cannot replicate or carry out metabolic processes on their own, as they lack the necessary cellular machinery. Thirdly, viruses do not always have DNA and can have RNA as their genetic material. Lastly, viruses are obligate intracellular parasites that require a host cell to reproduce and carry out their life cycle. While viruses exhibit some characteristics of life, they also possess attributes that challenge traditional definitions of what constitutes a living organism.

What is the function of a viral envelope?

It increases infectivity of the virus.


It holds the nucleic acids inside the virus.


It helps the virus attach to the host.

 

It allows the host to recognise the virus.


It replaces the capsid

The function of a viral envelope is to help the virus attach to the host cell. The envelope is a lipoprotein membrane derived from the host cell's plasma membrane or internal membrane. It contains viral glycoproteins that enable the virus to specifically recognize and bind to receptor molecules on the surface of the host cell. This attachment is essential for the virus to enter the host cell and initiate the infection process. Therefore, the viral envelope plays a crucial role in the initial stages of viral infection by facilitating the attachment and entry of the virus into the host cell.

The Minoans were primarily a trading empire and trade depends upon both organization and some kind of written records. What offered scholars the 'key' to unlocking the Minoan written language?

Linear A came from the Phoenician alphabet with which scholars were already familiar.


The many similarities between Linear A and Linear B made it relatively easy for scholars to understand and make sense of the Minoan language.

 

The Minoan written language was extremely similar to Egyptian hieroglyphs with which scholars were already familiar.


Although scholars were not familiar with Linear A, its later transformation into Linear B , the first writing system used by the Greeks, allowed scholars to gain a small understanding of what the Minoan language must have sounded like.

Although scholars were not familiar with Linear A, its later transformation into Linear B, the first writing system used by the Greeks, allowed scholars to gain a small understanding of what the Minoan language must have sounded like.

Sir Arthur Evans found Knossos, one of many palaces on the Island of Crete. The Knossos Palace consisted primarily of _____

a fort to protect the Minoan king from enemy invaders.


a private residence for the Minoan King.

 

a temple in which humans were sacrificed to the Minoan gods.


storage space for food.
The Knossos Palace consisted primarily of a private residence for the Minoan King.

Minoan civilization declined sharply around 1450 BCE because

All of the answers are possible


The volcanic island of Thera exploded, sending a tsunami that wiped out Minoan civilization

 

The advent of iron led to a decline in the need for bronze, which led to a decline in the need for tin, which in turn led to a decline in the need for the Minoan tin trade.


Mycenaeans from the Greek mainland began conquering Minoan culture

All of the answers are possible. 

The decline of Minoan civilization around 1450 BCE was likely influenced by multiple factors. The eruption of the volcano on the island of Thera (modern-day Santorini) is believed to have caused significant destruction and resulted in tsunamis that devastated Minoan settlements. The decline in the importance of bronze due to the rise of iron may have impacted the Minoan tin trade. Additionally, the Mycenaeans from mainland Greece gradually exerted influence and potentially conquered Minoan culture. These factors, along with others, likely contributed to the decline of the Minoan civilization.

The Minoan civilization reached its height during the bronze age. This coincides with their economy at the time because _____

they traded a lot of tin which when mixed with copper creates bronze.


they traded iron for bronze.

 

they grew a lot of expensive saffron which they were then able to trade for bronze.


their island had the natural resources required for making bronze.

The Minoan civilization reached its height during the Bronze Age because they traded a lot of tin. Tin, when mixed with copper, is used to create bronze. The Minoans had access to tin through trade, which allowed them to produce and trade bronze items. The combination of their trade networks and access to the necessary resources contributed to the development and prosperity of their Bronze Age civilization.

Minoan art has depictions of men in headdresses as well as men and women jumping over the end of a bull. In what way are these depictions similar to each other?

They offer scholars a clear understanding of everyday Minoan life.


In both instances, scholars are not entirely sure what the images represent.

 

They are typical of the red and white paintings seen in many Minoan murals.


They are illustrations of Minoan religious ceremonies.
In both instances, scholars are not entirely sure what the images represent.

Mycenae featured all of the following except:
Towers along the walls
A cyclopean wall
 
A megaron
A tunnel to an underground spring

Mycenae featured all of the options mentioned, so it did not exclude any of them. Mycenae had towers along the walls, a cyclopean wall (constructed with large, irregularly shaped stones), a megaron (a rectangular hall used for rulers' residences or ceremonial purposes), and a tunnel to an underground spring.

The heroes of later Greek myths were _____.
Minoan gods which the Greeks renamed
historical figures of the Mycenaean bronze age
 
Mycenaean gods, downgraded in the new pantheon
mythological figures adapted from old Minoan texts
The heroes of later Greek myths were mythological figures adapted from old Minoan texts.

Mycenae rose to power thanks to _____.
their unparalleled military skills
the sudden collapse and destruction of Troy
 
their superior technology
the power vacuum left by the Minoans

Mycenae rose to power thanks to the power vacuum left by the Minoans. The decline of the Minoan civilization created an opportunity for Mycenaeans to establish their dominance in the region. With the decline of Minoan power, Mycenae was able to rise and fill the void, becoming a major political and military force in the Bronze Age Aegean.

The Mycenaen civilization came to an end due to the following:
Sustained invasions from foreign peoples
Weather constraints and famine
 
A tsunami that shook up the entire Mediterranean
The rise of the Greek empire

The Mycenaean civilization came to an end due to sustained invasions from foreign peoples. Around the late 13th to early 12th century BCE, Mycenae and other Mycenaean cities faced invasions and attacks from various groups, including the Sea Peoples. These external invasions, combined with other factors such as internal unrest and possible economic decline, contributed to the collapse and ultimate end of the Mycenaean civilization.

Most archaeologists dislike Heinrich Schliemann because _____.
Schliemann destroyed a lot in his search.
Schliemann was wrong about the dating of layers.
 
They doubt that he actually found Troy and Mycenae.
They are jealous of his success.

Most archaeologists dislike Heinrich Schliemann because he destroyed a lot in his search. Schliemann was an amateur archaeologist who conducted excavations at Troy and Mycenae in the late 19th century. However, his excavation methods were not careful or meticulous, resulting in significant damage to the sites and the loss of valuable archaeological information. This lack of professional standards and disregard for preservation has led to a negative view of Schliemann among many archaeologists. While there may be varying opinions regarding his contributions and discoveries, the destruction caused during his excavations is a primary reason for the disapproval of his methods.

Understood! I'll provide concise and direct answers to your questions. Please go ahead and ask your question.

Mycenae featured all of the following except:
Towers along the walls
A cyclopean wall
 
A megaron
A tunnel to an underground spring
Mycenae featured all of the following, including towers along the walls, a cyclopean wall, a megaron, and a tunnel to an underground spring.
